mention_provenance.name: Directional Derivative
source_spans: For a unit direction u, the directional derivative is ∇f(x)·u, so the gradient points in the direction of steepest local increase.

mention_provenance.name: First-Order Taylor Approximation
source_spans: f(a+h) ≈ f(a) + ∇f(a)·h.

mention_provenance.name: Gradient
source_spans: The gradient ∇f(x) collects these partial derivatives into a vector.

mention_provenance.name: Gradient
source_spans: gradient \(\nabla f(x)\) describes local change

mention_provenance.name: Hessian Matrix
source_spans: the Hessian matrix, which describes local curvature.

mention_provenance.name: Partial Derivative
source_spans: A partial derivative measures how f changes when one coordinate changes while the other coordinates are held fixed.

mention_provenance.name: Stationary Point
source_spans: A point where the gradient is the zero vector is called a stationary point.

mention_provenance.name: Stationary Point
source_spans: stationary point is a point where the gradient is zero

mention_provenance.name: Taylor Approximation
source_spans: Taylor approximation describes the local behavior of a differentiable function near a point.

mention_provenance.name: Chain Rule
source_spans: The chain rule describes how the derivative of the composite function depends on the derivatives of f and g.

mention_provenance.name: Composite Function
source_spans: The composite function h = f ∘ g maps an input x ∈ ℝ^m to f(g(x)).

mention_provenance.name: Jacobian Matrix
source_spans: the Jacobian matrix J_g(x) collects all first-order partial derivatives of the output components of g.

mention_provenance.name: Basis
source_spans: A basis is a set of independent vectors that can represent every vector in the space through linear combinations.

mention_provenance.name: Characteristic Polynomial
source_spans: the characteristic polynomial det(λI - A)

mention_provenance.name: Dimension
source_spans: The number of vectors in a basis is the dimension of the vector space.

mention_provenance.name: Eigenvalue
source_spans: The corresponding eigenvalue λ satisfies Av = λ v.

mention_provenance.name: Eigenvalue Equation
source_spans: Av = λ v

mention_provenance.name: Eigenvector
source_spans: an eigenvector is a nonzero vector v whose direction is preserved by the transformation represented by A

mention_provenance.name: Linear Transformation
source_spans: a linear transformation

mention_provenance.name: Matrix
source_spans: A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers that can represent a linear transformation once bases have been chosen for the input and output spaces.

mention_provenance.name: Matrix Multiplication
source_spans: Matrix multiplication corresponds to composing linear transformations

mention_provenance.name: Vector Space
source_spans: A vector space is a set of objects called vectors that can be added together and multiplied by scalars while satisfying the vector space axioms.

mention_provenance.name: Dot Product
source_spans: the standard inner product is the dot product

mention_provenance.name: Inner Product
source_spans: An inner product is an operation that takes two vectors and returns a scalar.

mention_provenance.name: Least Squares Problem
source_spans: Least squares problems use projection ideas to solve inconsistent systems.

mention_provenance.name: Normal Equations
source_spans: A^\top A x = A^\top b.

mention_provenance.name: Orthogonal Projection
source_spans: Orthogonal projection is a method for finding the closest point in a subspace to a given vector.

mention_provenance.name: Orthogonal Vectors
source_spans: Two vectors are orthogonal when their inner product is zero.

mention_provenance.name: Projection Formula
source_spans: \operatorname{proj}_u(v) = \frac{v \cdot u}{u \cdot u}u.

mention_provenance.name: Convex Function
source_spans: convex functions

mention_provenance.name: Descent Direction
source_spans: negative gradient \(-\nabla f(x)\) is a descent direction

mention_provenance.name: Gradient Descent
source_spans: Gradient descent is a first-order iterative method that repeatedly moves in this direction

mention_provenance.name: Gradient Descent Update
source_spans: \[ x_{k+1} = x_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(x_k). \]

mention_provenance.name: Line Search
source_spans: A line search is a method for choosing a step size that gives sufficient decrease in the objective function

mention_provenance.name: Objective Function
source_spans: an objective function assigns a real value to each feasible input

mention_provenance.name: Optimisation Problem
source_spans: An optimisation problem asks for an input that makes the objective function as small or as large as possible

mention_provenance.name: Step Size
source_spans: scalar \(\alpha_k\) is the step size

mention_provenance.name: Unconstrained Minimisation Problem
source_spans: unconstrained minimisation problem

mention_provenance.name: Bayes' Rule
source_spans: P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)P(A)}{P(B)}.

mention_provenance.name: Conditional Probability
source_spans: Conditional probability measures the probability of an event given that another event has occurred.

mention_provenance.name: Conditional Probability Formula
source_spans: P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}.

mention_provenance.name: Expected Value
source_spans: The expected value of a random variable is a weighted average of its possible values.

mention_provenance.name: Expected Value Formula
source_spans: \mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_x x P(X=x).

mention_provenance.name: Probability Distribution
source_spans: The probability distribution of a random variable describes how probability is assigned to its possible values.

mention_provenance.name: Random Variable
source_spans: A random variable is a function that assigns a numerical value to each outcome of a random experiment.

mention_provenance.name: Variance
source_spans: Variance measures how far a random variable typically is from its expected value.

mention_provenance.name: Variance Formula
source_spans: \operatorname{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[(X-\mathbb{E}[X])^2].
